title: 'This is a great opportunity that we get to do': GS Athletics Day of Service - WJCL
content: Athletes, coaches and staff take part in GS Athletics Day of Service Athletes, coaches and staff take part in GS Athletics Day of Service Beginning of dialog window. Escape will cancel and close the window. Athletes, coaches and staff take part in GS Athletics Day of Service Georgia Southern athletes have been working hard on the fields and courts all season long. Now that hard work is extending beyond the playing fields, with those athletes taking time out of their schedule to give back to the Statesboro community. Georgia Southern softball players traded their bats and gloves for  different types of tools on Wednesday. Those players were just some of many from all of Eagle athletics to take part in the second annual Georgia Southern Athletics Day of Service. “We feel that by giving back and doing that, this is definitely a way to be a servant leader," said Gleen Hart, Ga. Southern senior associate athletics director for athletic development. "Being true from the heart. We also do a Feed the Boro event in February. We feed the homeless people here in the community. So just a part of our whole project of just giving back to those that help us on a year-round basis.” The athletes were joined by coaches and other athletic staff in performing charitable deeds at various venues throughout, and they’re glad to do it. “Think it's a great opportunity that we get to do this because we have so many people throughout the community who come and support us," said Bailey Holland, a GS senior softball pitcher. "So for us to be able to give back to them on this day is very special. Just, for all the support they give us, it's the least we can do.” Bailey and her teammates are helping build a Habitat for Humanity home, which is a big help to Habitat for Humanity Bulloch County. “Habitat, the vast majority of our labor is done through volunteers," said Aaron Marcinkevich, construction manager at Habitat for Humanity Bulloch County. "So every little bit helps. Even if people come out here with no prior construction knowledge. I'll teach them everything they need to do.” The Georgia Southern Athletics Day of Service has been a rousing success over these last two years. In total, 165 members of the Georgia Southern athletic department are taking part in this day of service event. "Typically, we had these events, it begins to fill up pretty quick with some sheets," said Hart. "It's not a matter of going around saying who can do it. It's a matter of, do we have enough spaces for them? So we're very excited about it. They love giving back, and it's kind of, they always ask me, 'Hey, Mr. Gleen, what's next?'" Some of the other places Georgia Southern athletics volunteered included senior citizen centers, schools and the food bank. Tariq Al-Desouki remains one of those familiar faces in Egyptian cinema and television whose work quietly accumulates over time. Born in 1957 in Qalyubiyya, he built a steady presence across film, TV series, and theater without chasing constant headlines. For viewers who appreciate character actors who deliver consistent performances, his body of work offers a window into the evolution of Egyptian drama from the 1980s onward. Many people first notice him in supporting roles that add texture to larger stories. His background includes a commerce degree from Ain Shams University, yet he chose acting as his path. This mix of formal education and artistic pursuit shows in performances that feel grounded rather than flashy. Whether playing everyday professionals or more layered figures, Tariq Al-Desouki brings a naturalism that fits the realistic tone common in much of Egyptian screen work. Tariq Al-Desouki is an Egyptian actor with over four decades of experience. He appeared in notable titles such as The Talisman (1987) and Al Awbash (1986), among many others. His filmography covers a range of genres, though he often finds himself in dramatic or social commentary pieces that reflect Egyptian society. What sets his contributions apart is reliability. Directors and casting teams have turned to him repeatedly because he can inhabit roles without drawing undue attention to himself. In an industry where lead actors sometimes dominate the frame, supporting performers like Tariq Al-Desouki help anchor scenes and make the overall narrative feel authentic. His education in commerce might seem unrelated to acting at first glance. Yet it likely gave him an understanding of structure and discipline that translates well to the demands of long production schedules. Actors who treat their craft professionally, showing up prepared and consistent, tend to sustain longer careers. Tariq Al-Desouki fits that profile. Tariq Al-Desouki has also participated in theater, a medium that requires different skills from on-camera work. Live performance hones timing and audience awareness in ways that benefit screen acting later. Many Egyptian actors move fluidly between stage and screen, and his path reflects that tradition. Watching actors like Tariq Al-Desouki offers viewers a chance to study subtle craft. His performances reward close attention. You notice how he uses small gestures or shifts in expression to convey internal conflict. For film students or enthusiasts interested in acting technique, his body of work provides practical examples without the gloss of stardom. On a personal level, discovering his older films can feel like uncovering layers of cultural history. Egyptian cinema from the 1980s and 1990s captured social changes, economic realities, and family dynamics in ways that still resonate. Engaging with his roles gives context to those periods without requiring academic analysis. That said, drawbacks exist. Much of his output appears in older productions that may not have high-definition restorations or easy streaming access outside specific regions. Language barriers also play a role for non-Arabic speakers, as subtitles vary in quality. Availability can frustrate international audiences trying to build a complete viewing list. Another limitation involves the supporting nature of many roles. While this allows breadth, it sometimes means less screen time to develop complex character arcs. Viewers seeking deep character studies in every performance might feel occasional disappointment when a role serves mainly to advance the plot. A quick personal note from experience: years ago I recommended a Tariq Al-Desouki film to a friend new to Egyptian cinema. I highlighted his steady presence, thinking it would ease her into the style. Instead, she found the pacing slower than expected and struggled with cultural references. We learned together that context matters more than any single actor's contribution. Starting with more accessible contemporary series before diving into classics often works better for newcomers. Public records and databases like IMDb list Tariq Al-Desouki with credits stretching back decades. Exact viewership numbers for older Egyptian productions prove difficult to verify consistently, but his repeated casting suggests industry recognition. Film historians and Egyptian media archives note that actors who maintain long careers often do so through versatility and professionalism rather than viral moments. Available interviews and public appearances show Tariq Al-Desouki discussing the craft in measured terms, focusing on collaboration and preparation. Recent news mentions a return to Ramadan drama after a reported 14-year gap, with the series Ali Clay. Such comebacks highlight the cyclical nature of acting careers, especially in television seasons tied to cultural events like Ramadan. Data from streaming platforms and broadcast ratings (where available) indicate that established supporting actors can still draw audiences when paired with strong leads or timely stories. Evidence remains somewhat anecdotal rather than comprehensive. Comprehensive academic studies on individual supporting actors are rare compared to leads or directors. What exists points to a pattern: longevity correlates with adaptability across changing production standards, audience tastes, and industry shifts. Mixed signals appear when comparing television versus film work. Television often provides steadier employment but faces criticism for formulaic writing. Film roles, though fewer, sometimes allow more nuanced performances. Tariq Al-Desouki navigated both, which speaks to practical career management over singular artistic statements. He has a BA in Business from Metro State University and a Public Leadership Credential from Harvard’s Kennedy School of Government. He was the original host of Talking Drum, the signature current affairs show on the African Broadcasting Network (ABN-America), which was available nationwide in the United States via the Dish Network satellite service. On the show, he interviewed Nobel laureates such as 2004 Nobel Peace prize winner, Professor Wangari Maathai, the first woman from Africa to win the peace prize and heads of states. Tom has served and chaired various boards including Global Minnesota (formerly Minnesota International Center), the sixth largest World Affairs Council in the United States. He has previously served as the first Black President of the Board of Directors at Books for Africa. He also serves on the boards of New Vision Foundation and the Minnesota Multicultural Media Consortium. He has previously served two terms on the board of the United Nations Association. An avid runner, he retired from running full marathons after turning 50 and now only focuses on training for half marathons. title: More than paper: The Buick Heritage Alliance also preserves cars - Old Cars Weekly
content: The Buick Heritage Alliance (BHA) formed more than 20 years ago with one mission: the preservation of all things Buick. For people who love Buicks, the BHA will help research their Buicks so they can be restored and preserved. The BHA collects, compiles, catalogues, and shares information on the history of Buick and those who contributed to its development. The BHA is best known for its impressive collection of Buick literature, now housed at the AACA Library and Research Center in Hershey, Pa. Anyone may access the collection via the BHA website at  But the BHA’s preservation efforts also include several historically important Buick automobiles. In the fall of 1969, two identical 1970 GS Stage 1 Buicks fresh off the assembly line were turned into stunning show cars for the Chicago Auto Show. One became the well-known GSX show car, and the other is this one-of-a-kind eye-catching coupe in Fireglow Red Pearl with a pure white interior. Both caused a sensation during their debut at the Chicago Auto Show and on the auto show circuit. They luckily escaped the crusher when noted Columbus, Ohio, Buick dealer Len Immke convinced General Motors to sell the cars to him, with the agreement that he would not resell them. A few years later, they were, in fact, both sold. This Fireglow example ended up in the hands of racer Ed Balling, who immediately installed 4.30:1 rear-end ratio gears. He retired the GS in 1978 after its engine had been blown. In 2003, it was purchased and restored by John Chamberlain using a correct-code and year-date engine block, fitted with the car’s original cylinder heads, carburetor, distributor and pistons. He also re-installed the original 3.64:1 gear set. The cracked paint was re-sprayed to the correct color. It then passed through a succession of owners before recently being donated to the Buick Heritage Alliance. During its time through multiple owners, the pure-white carpet was replaced with standard black cut-pile. Between December 2024 and November 2025, the BHA invested in correctly restoring the car to its original, as-shown-in-1970 condition. Thanks to enthusiast-professionals Ajesh Parikh, Adam Martin and Jason Walls for their work on the restoration. This GS is highly optioned with a Stage 1 455-cid V-8 pumping out 360 hp and 510 lb.-ft. of torque coupled to a Turbo Hydra-matic automatic transmission and a 3.64 positive-traction rear end. Options include power steering, disc brakes, power windows and locks, AM-FM radio, 15x7 five-spoked chrome-plated wheels and Goodyear Polyglas G60-15 GT tires. It has absolutely clear window glass, because any window tint would cast a blue glow on the white interior. In November 2025, the freshly restored Fireglow Red Pearl GS showcar debuted at the prestigious Muscle Car and Corvette Nationals in Rosemont, Ill., drawing crowds of enthusiasts throughout the weekend. This unique 1970 GS is now on display at NATMUS (National Auto & Truck Museum) in Auburn, Ind. The eighth-generation Buick Riviera was introduced for the 1995 model year and was immediately recognized for its ride, comfort, power and styling. Although a convertible was not initially planned for production, the Riviera and platform mate Oldsmobile Aurora were built with world-class stiffness, leading GM to study the idea of a convertible Riviera. Reportedly three test cars may have been built, but this is the only known survivor. (Rumors have persisted over the years that the convertible was the work of a rogue GM employee, who was subsequently fired after this convertible concept car was discovered, but that story was debunked by reliable GM sources.) Thanks to a generous donation by member Len Woelfel, this unique and striking one-of-a-kind Riviera is now part of the Buick Heritage Alliance collection. In a rare color of Dark Cherry Metallic, it has all the hallmarks of a working convertible, but no top was fitted, and the tonneau cover does not open. Powered by a supercharged V-6 offering 225 hp and 275 lb.-ft. of torque, it is front-wheel drive and sits on special 17-in. wheels, slightly larger than stock 16-in. wheels. The platinum beige interior with wood trim is stock, as are the instruments and controls. Showing slightly more than 2,700 miles, this unique chapter in Buick history is maintained by the BHA so Buick enthusiasts of today and the future can appreciate the style and grace of this unique model from Buick history. You can also see this car in person at NATMUS in Auburn, Ind. — next to the 1970 GS show car. Despite the ongoing depression in America, the Buicks of 1937 debuted as bigger, better and more beautiful than before. In fact, this Model 40C Special five-passenger convertible sedan was a new model for the year. Buick produced nearly 1,700 of them for domestic and export sales. The BHA’s example was in a sad state before undergoing extensive restoration, and today delights with its art deco interior, tear-drop headlamps and a split grille that is considered a masterpiece of design. Other highlights include the highly desirable dual sidemount spare tires, dual side mirrors, white sidewall tires and a factory radio. Its 248-cid, 100-hp overhead-valve straight-eight engine accelerates with authority, and its four-wheel hydraulic brakes perform flawlessly. From the beginning, the name “Skylark” meant something special at Buick. In 1953, it was a full-size flagship for Buick, a Motorama show car come to life. In 1961, Skylark became Buick’s import-fighting small car, but in 1964, it evolved into a line of popular midsize cars that were a sales bonanza. This 1965 Skylark convertible, part of the BHA’s collection of significant Buicks, is a stunning award-winning restoration. Its Wildcat 310 engine carries a reputation for spirited performance by displacing 300 cubic inches with a Rochester two-barrel carburetor and 9.0:1 compression. This car has Buick’s Super Turbine 300 automatic transmission, four-wheel drum brakes, 14-in. chrome-plated five-spoke wheels and a bench seat with medium-blue upholstery. Its restoration preserves the car as new. This car was donated to the BHA after the passing of long-time BCA member, and former president, Al Eichelberger. His parents purchased it new, and it was his mother’s pride and joy. Though other cars came and went through Al’s family, Al saw to it that this never left. It holds a treasured spot in the BHA’s collection. Throughout its 20-year history, the BHA has been powered by the generosity of the Buick community for donations to fund its ongoing work of collecting and sharing Buick literature, and more recently, preserving historically significant Buick automobiles. You can help support this important work by donating literature, funds or even a Buick vehicle to the BHA. As a 501c3 corporation, your donation may well be tax deductible. Learn more at www.buickheritagealliance.org or contact BHA President Jeff Brashares at usa.jbrashares@cma-cgm.com, or call 614-361-7299. title: Will Goldman (GS) Beat Estimates Again in Its Next Earnings Report? - Yahoo Finance Australia
content: Tip: Try a valid symbol or a specific company name for relevant results Have you been searching for a stock that might be well-positioned to maintain its earnings-beat streak in its upcoming report? It is worth considering Goldman Sachs (GS), which belongs to the Zacks Financial - Investment Bank industry. This investment bank has seen a nice streak of beating earnings estimates, especially when looking at the previous two reports. The average surprise for the last two quarters was 14.65%. For the most recent quarter, Goldman was expected to post earnings of $11.77 per share, but it reported $14.01 per share instead, representing a surprise of 19.03%. For the previous quarter, the consensus estimate was $11.11 per share, while it actually produced $12.25 per share, a surprise of 10.26%. With this earnings history in mind, recent estimates have been moving higher for Goldman. In fact, the Zacks Earnings ESP (Expected Surprise Prediction) for the company is positive, which is a great sign of an earnings beat, especially when you combine this metric with its nice Zacks Rank. Our research shows that stocks with the combination of a positive Earnings ESP and a Zacks Rank #3 (Hold) or better produce a positive surprise nearly 70% of the time. In other words, if you have 10 stocks with this combination, the number of stocks that beat the consensus estimate could be as high as seven. The Zacks Earnings ESP compares the Most Accurate Estimate to the Zacks Consensus Estimate for the quarter; the Most Accurate Estimate is a version of the Zacks Consensus whose definition is related to change. The idea here is that analysts revising their estimates right before an earnings release have the latest information, which could potentially be more accurate than what they and others contributing to the consensus had predicted earlier. Goldman currently has an Earnings ESP of +5.02%, which suggests that analysts have recently become bullish on the company's earnings prospects. This positive Earnings ESP when combined with the stock's Zacks Rank #3 (Hold) indicates that another beat is possibly around the corner. We expect the company's next earnings report to be released on April 13, 2026. With the Earnings ESP metric, it's important to note that a negative value reduces its predictive power; however, a negative Earnings ESP does not indicate an earnings miss. Many companies end up beating the consensus EPS estimate, but that may not be the sole basis for their stocks moving higher. On the other hand, some stocks may hold their ground even if they end up missing the consensus estimate. Because of this, it's really important to check a company's Earnings ESP ahead of its quarterly release to increase the odds of success. title: Silva Scores 33 as GS Caltex Takes Game 1 in Championship Finals - 서울경제
content: Women's Pro Volleyball Championship Opens with 3-1 Victory · Regular Season Leaders Korea Expressway Corp. Stumble Under New Coaching GS Caltex successfully seized the initiative against regular season top-ranked Korea Expressway Corporation in the women's professional volleyball championship finals. GS Caltex defeated Korea Expressway Corp. 3-1 (25-23, 23-25, 25-15, 25-22) in Game 1 of the Jin Air 2025-2026 V-League women's division championship series (best-of-five) held at Gimcheon Gymnasium on the 1st. Historically, teams that won Game 1 in the women's best-of-five championship finals have gone on to win the title 11 out of 19 times, a 57.9 percent rate. GS Caltex, which finished the regular season in third place, defeated Heungkuk Life Insurance in the single-elimination semi-playoff and swept Hyundai E&C 2-0 in the best-of-three playoff to reach the championship finals for the first time in five years since the 2020-2021 season. GS Caltex extended its postseason winning streak to four matches, taking Game 1 against Korea Expressway Corp., which had decided to terminate the contract of former head coach Kim Jong-min ahead of the championship series. Riding strong momentum, GS Caltex took the first set against a Korea Expressway Corp. side that had been dealing with internal turmoil. At the center of the effort was GS Caltex's main attacker Silva, who scored 11 points in the first set alone. GS Caltex struggled with its receive early in the set and could not fully utilize Silva's attacking power. However, the team found stability from the middle of the set and began to close the gap. Trailing 16-20, GS Caltex won the serve with Silva's quick open attack, then Silva scored three consecutive points during captain Yoo Seo-yeon's serve to level the score. At 21-21, Silva converted a difficult open attack to give her team the lead for the first time. Kwon Min-ji then blocked an attack by Moma Letizia Bassoco (registered name: Moma), extending the lead to 23-21. GS Caltex clinched the first set when Reina Dococu (registered name: Reina), who entered via a double substitution at 24-23, successfully landed an attack. The second set went to Korea Expressway Corp., as Kim Se-bin effectively neutralized Silva's attacks like a leech. Kim Se-bin recorded four blocking points in the second set alone, all against Silva's attacks. Trailing 6-11, Korea Expressway Corp. turned the momentum with Kim Se-bin's block and scored three additional consecutive points to pull within one. At 20-20, Korea Expressway Corp. took the lead with Moma's quick open, and Kim Se-bin again blocked Silva's attack. Silva, who had struggled throughout the second set, committed a service error at 23-24, sending the match back to even at one set apiece. GS Caltex regrouped and seized control from the start of the third set, with balanced contributions from Kwon Min-ji, Yoo Seo-yeon, and Choi Ga-eun. The team spared Silva's shoulder and built a commanding 20-10 lead. Late in the set, GS Caltex used a double substitution, replacing Silva and Ahn Hye-jin with Reina and Kim Ji-won. GS Caltex closed out the match in the fourth set. Trailing 16-18 through the middle of the set, the team surged to a 21-18 lead on the back of two consecutive points from Kwon Min-ji, an error by Moma, and a block by Silva. After a back-and-forth battle, Yoo Seo-yeon delivered a quick open attack that caught the opposing blockers off guard at 23-22, reaching match point at 24-22. Silva then sealed the victory with a powerful back-row attack. Silva showcased her dominance with 33 points and a 49.21 percent attack success rate. Kwon Min-ji (14 points) and Yoo Seo-yeon (13 points) provided double-digit scoring support. For Korea Expressway Corp., Moma fought valiantly with 31 points, but no domestic player reached double-digit scoring. The two teams will play Game 2 of the championship series at the same venue on the 3rd. AI-translated from Korean. Quotes from foreign sources are based on Korean-language reports and may not reflect exact original wording.

title: Will Goldman Sachs (GS) beat quarterly earnings? Predicti... - Polymarket
content: Trader sentiment on Polymarket prices a 91% implied probability that Goldman Sachs (GS) will beat Q1 2026 EPS consensus of approximately $16.00, reflecting the firm's streak of surpassing estimates in the last four quarters, capped by Q4 2025's record $4.31 billion equities trading revenue and 12% FICC growth to $3.11 billion amid heightened volatility. Recent analyst estimate revisions upward, fueled by sustained market turbulence from tariff talks and equity swings favoring GS's trading franchise, underpin this strong conviction ahead of the April 13 earnings release. While macro tailwinds persist, risks include softer investment banking fees if M&A activity stalls or surprise credit provisions, potentially challenging the beat. Ahead of the championship series, Korea Expressway Corporation (KEC), which decided to part ways with coach Kim Jong-min, went into the first game with senior coach Kim Young-rae serving as acting head coach but suffered a painful defeat. title: GS Caltex Wins Championship Finals Opener vs Korea Expressway Corporation - 조선일보
content: Third-place GS Caltex leads 1-0 in best-of-five finals with 57.9% title chance GS Caltex, which defeated Hyundai Engineering & Construction in the playoffs, continued its formidable upward trajectory by defeating the regular-season champion Korea Expressway Corporation in the first game of the championship finals. GS Caltex secured its first victory in the 2025-2026 V-League Women’s Championship Finals on the 1st, defeating Korea Expressway Corporation 3-1 (25-23, 23-25, 25-15, 25-22) at the Kimcheon Indoor Gymnasium, the home court of Korea Expressway Corporation. In the history of the best-of-five championship finals, the team that wins the first game has a 57.9% chance of claiming the title. The first set saw GS Caltex, riding high from its playoff victory, extend its momentum. Korea Expressway Corporation initially led, but GS Caltex gradually closed the gap to trail by just one point at 13-12. When Korea Expressway Corporation pulled ahead 20-16 on a series of attacks by Moma and Kang So-hwi, GS Caltex’s Gisele Silva responded with consecutive points to narrow the gap to 20-19. GS Caltex then capitalized on two consecutive blocked attacks by Moma, with Silva’s back-to-back points helping them take a 22-21 lead. Kwon Min-ji sealed the set with a block on Moma’s attack, securing the 25-23 victory. The second set unfolded in the opposite fashion. GS Caltex started strong, with Kwon Min-ji and Silva’s consecutive points giving them an 11-6 lead. However, Korea Expressway Corporation rallied as Kim Se-bin and Moma’s successful blocks, followed by Moma’s consecutive points, narrowed the gap to 11-12. A attacking error by Kwon Min-ji led to a 12-12 tie, and the teams battled to 20-20. Korea Expressway Corporation eventually took control, capitalizing on a quick attack reception and Moma’s point to reverse the score to 21-20. Although GS Caltex’s substitute Reina scored twice to tie it at 22-22, Moma’s consecutive points gave Korea Expressway Corporation the set point at 24-22, and a service error by Silva concluded the set 25-23. In the third set, Korea Expressway Corporation collapsed abruptly due to unforced errors and lapses in concentration, allowing GS Caltex to easily claim the set. Despite Korea Expressway Corporation’s attempts to regain momentum, GS Caltex dominated with Kwon Min-ji’s standout performance, solid reception, blocking, and balanced scoring from the team. They built an 11-point lead at 22-11 and closed the set 25-15, taking a 2-1 lead in the set score. The decisive fourth set saw a tight battle from the start between the cornered Korea Expressway Corporation and GS Caltex. Kim Se-in and Silva traded fierce attacks. At 18-18, a attacking error by Moma gave GS Caltex a 19-18 lead. Silva’s block and subsequent point extended the advantage to 21-18. Korea Expressway Corporation narrowed the gap to 22-21 with Lee Ye-eun’s sharp serves and Moma’s points, but GS Caltex’s Yu Seo-yeon delivered a critical point at 23-22 to reach match point at 24-22. Silva then sealed the victory with the final point, securing the 1-0 lead in the championship finals. With this win, regular-season third-place GS Caltex has taken a favorable position in the championship race. Silva shone despite knee pain, scoring 33 points, while Kwon Min-ji (14 points) and Yu Seo-yeon (13 points) delivered crucial scores in key moments. On the other hand, Korea Expressway Corporation, which effectively dismissed its long-time coach Kim Jong-min ahead of the championship finals, saw acting coach Kim Young-rae struggle to steady the team during critical moments. The third set’s sudden loss of focus proved costly. Moma fought hard with 31 points, but Kang So-hwi’s limited contribution of 7 points was a notable shortfall.

title: Thermo Fisher Scientific Launches Gibco CHOvantage GS Cell Line Development Kit To Accelerate Biologics Development - biopharmaapac.com
content: Gibco™ CHOvantage™ GS Cell Line Development Kit delivers high productivity, streamlined timelines and royalty-free, clinical stage licensing options to support scalable manufacturing. Gibco CHOvantage GS Cell Line Development Kit delivers high productivity, streamlined timelines and royalty-free, clinical-stage licensing options to support scalable manufacturing Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., the world leader in serving science,  announced a next-generation, integrated cell line development platform that enables biologics developers to accelerate time to clinic while maintaining regulatory confidence and commercial scalability. Global pipelines for protein therapeutics, including complex biologics and biosimilars used to treat conditions ranging from cancer to autoimmune and rare diseases, continue to expand. This growth is placing increasing pressure on manufacturers to shorten time to clinic, improve manufacturing consistency and control cost of goods without compromising quality or regulatory compliance. At the same time, regulatory agencies are placing greater emphasis on demonstrating product quality and comparability to innovator molecules. The Gibco™ CHOvantage™ GS Cell Line Development (CLD) Kit enables researchers to generate high-performing, CHO cell lines, achieving protein titers of ≥7 g/L in fed-batch cultures, establishing stable pools in as little as four weeks, and supporting selection of stable clones within 14 weeks, helping to streamline progression from early development to commercial manufacturing. “Biologics developers face increasing pressure to deliver therapies faster while managing cost, quality and regulatory complexity,” said Sara Henneman, vice president and general manager of Thermo Fisher Scientific’s cell culture and cell therapy business. “With the CHOvantage GS CLD Kit, we’re helping customers compress development timelines, increase confidence in quality and lower overall costs through a royalty-free, clinical-stage licensing model that helps minimize barriers to market. This platform reflects our commitment to simplifying and standardizing biologics development workflows from research through commercial production.” Patent expirations are accelerating the development of lower-cost biosimilar medicines, increasing demand for reliable and scalable technologies that can help manufacturers bring these treatments to market more quickly while maintaining strict quality and regulatory standards. Integrated development platforms can help simplify complex manufacturing processes, shorten development timelines and support consistent large-scale production. “The CHOvantage GS CLD Kit is our newest example of how we are advancing cell line productivity and process consistency across the biologics lifecycle,” said Andy Campbell, senior director of research and development at Thermo Fisher Scientific. “By integrating a transposon-based vector system with our Efficient-Pro media and feeds, we’re giving customers a reliable, high-quality, streamlined platform that helps to save time, reduces variability and supports scalability. Importantly, this harmonized workflow is designed to simplify tech transfer and enable a smoother transition from development to manufacturing.” BioFactura (a developer and manufacturer of high-value biosimilars and novel drugs) was a key pre-launch testing partner for the Gibco CHOvantage GS Cell Line Development Kit. Lead Cell Line Development Scientist Carson Brackna stated, "Combining transposon vectors and optimized media and feeds, the CHOvantage platform offered a robust, end-to-end cell line package while remaining user-friendly and yielding quality protein with high productivity on a tight schedule.” Simplified Licensing, No Clinical-Stage RoyaltiesMany cell line development systems require royalties or milestone payments during the clinical stage. The CHOvantage GS CLD Kit includes research-use rights with purchase, avoiding these common fees during development. Royalty-free, clinical-stage licensing options provide greater cost predictability and commercial flexibility, enabling a more seamless transition from development through full-scale production. GS Engineering & Construction operates as a leading South Korean firm in the engineering, procurement, and construction (EPC) sector, with significant exposure to plant projects, housing development, and emerging energy technologies. The company, traded on the Korea Exchange under ISIN KR7006360002 in Korean won, focuses on high-value infrastructure that positions it for long-term growth in Asia's dynamic markets. By Elena Voss, Senior Financial Editor at NorthStar Market Insights: GS Engineering & Const exemplifies South Korea's push into sustainable infrastructure, blending traditional construction strengths with next-generation energy solutions. GS Engineering & Construction's business spans EPC services for plants, civil engineering, housing, and power projects. It leverages expertise in petrochemicals, LNG facilities, and now eco-friendly energy to serve domestic and international clients. This diversified model helps mitigate risks from cyclical construction markets. The firm's plant division handles complex projects like LNG terminals and chemical plants, drawing on decades of experience in Northeast Asia. Housing operations focus on urban redevelopment and new complexes, capitalizing on South Korea's real estate demand. Such segmentation supports steady revenue streams across economic cycles. In competitive terms, GS E&C ranks among South Korea's top constructors, competing with firms like Samsung C&T and POSCO E&C. Its edge lies in integrated EPC capabilities and growing international bids, particularly in the Middle East and Vietnam. A key growth area involves hydrogen technologies, where GS E&C advances liquid hydrogen storage solutions. The company participates in national projects to develop storage tanks and drip chamber systems, marking Korea's first flat-floor tank technology. This positions it at the forefront of the hydrogen economy. Partnerships with entities like Korea Gas Corporation and Japan's Ebara enhance its EPC capabilities for hydrogen infrastructure. These efforts shift the portfolio from petrochemicals to renewables, aligning with global decarbonization trends. Future expansions could include large-scale storage demonstrations. Such initiatives tap into South Korea's hydrogen strategy, potentially unlocking government-backed funding and international contracts. For investors, this diversification reduces reliance on traditional construction amid energy transitions worldwide. GS E&C actively bids on nuclear power plant projects, including sites in the Middle East and Vietnam like Tim Korea. These high-margin ventures complement its plant expertise, with analysts noting cost stabilization in LNG and chemical sites. Data centers represent another pillar, driven by Asia's digital boom. The company's modular development and overseas urban projects broaden its scope beyond domestic markets. Reorganization post-asset sales emphasizes these value-added segments. Expected financial improvements stem from sequential cost reductions in legacy projects and new business inflows. Operating profit projections highlight plant sector resilience, bolstering overall profitability. In housing, GS E&C leads projects like Mokdong Hansin Chunggu remodeling, increasing units from 332 to 361 with larger sizes. Target move-in is May 2031, reflecting strong demand in Seoul's premium areas. Other developments include Asan Tangjeongzai Metro City, offering competitive financing to attract buyers. These initiatives benefit from South Korea's urban renewal policies and rising property values. Unit prices in similar complexes have appreciated notably over the past year. The housing arm provides stable cash flows, balancing volatile plant projects. Government support for redevelopment sustains pipeline momentum, with HUG guarantees enhancing sales stability. title: Taean expo partners with GS Retail to boost promotion across South Korea - CHOSUNBIZ - Chosunbiz
content: The Taean International Horticultural Healing Expo Organizing Committee said on Apr. 1 that it signed a business agreement with GS Retail to strengthen promotion for this year's expo. Under the agreement, the committee will support the production of promotional videos and images and offer up to a 40% discount on admission tickets for GS Retail executives and employees. It will also provide space to operate a temporary convenience store at the venue during the expo. GS Retail will promote the expo by airing promotional videos at stores in South Chungcheong, Daejeon, and Sejong, posting posters, and attaching promotional materials to store facades. An organizing committee official said, "Through this agreement, we have created an opportunity for more people to experience the expo," and added, "We will also strengthen on-site convenience services to make it an expo that satisfies visitors." Meanwhile, the 2026 Taean International Horticultural Healing Expo will be held for one month from Apr. 25 to May 24 at the Kkotji Coastal Park area on Anmyeondo in Taean County. This year's event, themed "A healthy future of horticulture and healing found in nature," will be co-hosted by South Chungcheong Province and Taean County. The organizers expected 1.82 million visitors from 40 countries this year.

title: Kim Seong-won boosts GS Global with 40,000-share buy, vows value growth in Korea - CHOSUNBIZ - Chosunbiz
content: GS Global said on the 1st that new CEO (president) Kim Sung-won acquired 40,000 treasury shares through on-exchange purchases immediately after the regular shareholders meeting on the 26th of last month. The purchase price of the shares is about 1 billion won. GS Global said the chief executive's purchase of company shares signaled confidence in mid- to long-term growth and a commitment to resolve the undervaluation of corporate value. Key executives and employees also plan to voluntarily participate in share purchases. Kim said, "This decision is based on confidence in the company's future growth potential," adding, "We will continue to work to enhance shareholder value and practice responsible management." Kim previously served as head of overseas energy business at GS Energy and as CEO of GS E&R, and was appointed CEO of GS Global at the regular shareholders meeting on the 26th of last month. In addition, GS Global revamped its management structure. CEO Kim and Chief Financial Officer (CFO) Kim Jae-sung were newly appointed as inside directors, while other non-executive director Kim Seok-hwan and outside director Wi Sung-ho were reappointed. Outside director Shin Chang-dong was newly appointed. GS Global plans to diversify its business portfolio and secure new growth engines to change its sales structure, which is concentrated in the trade and distribution segment. This year's sales target is about 4.6 trillion won, higher than last year's sales (4.1093 trillion won). GS Global will also continue its shareholder return policy. On the 3rd, it plans to pay a cash dividend of 25 won per share and will continue to enhance shareholder value through stable dividends. We use cookies to enhance your experience and analyze traffic. title: GS Warriors Vs Washington Wizards Full Game Highlights | 01/24/2019 NBA Season [64dd61] - Fathom Journal
content:
Join over 9000 global opinion formers by subscribing to Fathom's influential Weekly Newsletter Xbox Game Pass offers a subscription model that gives access to hundreds of games across consoles, PC, and cloud streaming. For many players, it has shifted how they discover and play titles without committing full price upfront. Whether you lean toward single-player adventures, multiplayer sessions, or quick mobile picks, the service promises variety at a fixed cost. As of early 2026, the lineup includes updated tiers following a major overhaul in late 2025. Prices rose notably for the top plan, and the structure now splits into Essential, Premium, Ultimate, and a separate PC Game Pass. This article breaks down the current state, real benefits weighed against drawbacks, supporting data, and a clear path to decide if it fits your setup. At its heart, Xbox Game Pass functions like a streaming service for video games. Subscribers gain entry to a rotating catalog that spans genres, from big-budget releases to smaller indies. Microsoft continues to add first-party titles, often on launch day for higher tiers, alongside partner content. The service covers Xbox consoles, Windows PCs, and cloud gaming on supported devices like phones, tablets, or smart TVs. You download or stream titles without buying them individually. Perks often include member discounts on purchases, in-game rewards, and bundled services in premium plans. Core value sits in reducing the risk of buying a game that falls flat. Instead of dropping $70 on something untested, you try it through the subscription. This appeals especially to players who rotate through many titles or have limited budgets for new releases. Families or households with multiple gamers also benefit from one account covering several devices. That said, the library size varies by tier. Lower plans offer curated selections or delayed access to newer releases. Higher ones deliver more immediate entry and extras like EA Play or Ubisoft+ Classics. Benefits show up clearest for active gamers who finish or sample several games each month. Day-one access in Ultimate means jumping into new Xbox studios or Bethesda releases without waiting. Cloud gaming adds flexibility for travel or when the main TV is occupied. Perks such as store credits through Rewards can offset part of the fee for dedicated users. Multiplayer stays included across plans, along with cloud options that have improved to 1440p in the top tier. PC integration allows cross-save and play-anywhere features for many titles. For someone averaging 20-30 hours monthly across different games, the math often favors the subscription over individual buys. Drawbacks emerge in practice. Games rotate in and out, so a favorite might leave before you finish it. The 2025 price jump for Ultimate to $29.99 per month crossed a threshold for some long-time subscribers, making annual costs approach $360 before tax. Lower tiers delay or limit first-party access, which frustrates players wanting the latest hits right away. Internet dependency hits cloud users hard during peak hours or on slower connections. Library curation sometimes buries gems under volume, leading to decision fatigue. Ownership remains absent; cancel the subscription and access ends. I learned this the hard way a couple years back. I had stacked several promising titles in my queue during a busy work stretch, figuring I would knock them out over weekends. One long-awaited RPG finally hit the service, and I dove in eagerly. Two weeks later, real life intervened with a family trip and deadlines. By the time I returned, the game had already cycled out of the lower tier I was on. I ended up buying it at full price anyway, annoyed at the wasted anticipation. The lesson stuck: treat the library as a sampler, not a permanent backlog. Subscriber numbers provide one lens. Estimates placed the base around 35-37 million in early 2025, with some reports pushing toward 40 million by Q1 2026. Microsoft stopped publishing exact public figures after 2024 disclosures, but revenue from content and services, which includes Game Pass, has shown steady if uneven growth. FY2025 reportedly generated nearly $5 billion from the service, with analysts projecting continued contributions to overall Xbox revenue. User sentiment mixes appreciation for variety with complaints about value after price changes. Forums and reviews highlight strong retention among heavy players who exploit day-one drops and cloud features. Lighter users often question the cost once they exhaust initial discovery phases. Independent analyses note that average revenue per user has risen with tier adjustments and add-ons. However, growth has not always matched Microsoft's earlier ambitious targets for subscriber scale. Hardware sales have softened in periods, shifting emphasis toward services. Evidence on completion rates or "value realized" stays anecdotal but consistent: players who treat it as a discovery tool report higher satisfaction than those expecting a complete replacement for buying games. Cloud performance data from tech outlets shows meaningful gains in resolution and latency for Ultimate, though real-world results depend on local infrastructure. Data remains mixed on long-term retention. Some users churn after binge periods, while others maintain subscriptions for years by pacing play across family members or devices. Begin by assessing your current hardware and habits. Do you own an Xbox console, a capable Windows PC, or rely mainly on mobile? Answers guide tier choice. Visit the official Xbox site or app to compare plans. New users often qualify for promotional pricing, such as a discounted first month or two weeks. Sign in with a Microsoft account, select your tier, and link payment details. The app or console dashboard then surfaces the library. Download the Xbox app on PC or use the built-in store on console. For cloud play, test compatibility on your preferred device. Start with a lower tier if unsure, then upgrade later if needed. Most changes take effect immediately or at the next billing cycle. Track your playtime and library additions via the Rewards program or dashboard stats. Set a simple rule: sample three new games per month to justify the fee. Adjust based on actual usage after the first billing period. Choosing the right plan requires matching features to needs. Here is a breakdown of current Xbox options alongside major alternatives. Ultimate delivers the broadest reach but at the highest ongoing cost. Premium strikes a middle ground for many, especially those patient with release timing. Essential works for budget-focused multiplayer sessions. Competitors like PlayStation Plus emphasize owned-platform depth and classics, while Nintendo stays affordable for handheld retro play but lacks the scale of modern libraries. Cross-platform flexibility gives Xbox an edge for mixed-device households. Price sensitivity after recent hikes narrows the gap with individual purchases for selective players. One frequent issue is subscription creep. Users sign up during hype, play heavily for a month, then let the service run unused. Set calendar reminders to review usage before renewal. Cancel and resubscribe during sales or when a big release drops if your pattern is seasonal. Another trap involves ignoring rotation schedules. Check the "leaving soon" section regularly and prioritize unfinished games. Download favorites locally when possible to extend access. Decision paralysis from library size leads to shallow play. Curate a shortlist of 5-10 titles based on genre preferences or community recommendations. Focus on one or two at a time. Technical hiccups, such as download failures or cloud lag, frustrate newcomers. Ensure stable internet, update the app or console, and clear cache periodically. Test cloud on a wired connection first. Over-reliance on the service can reduce willingness to buy standout titles you want to own permanently. Balance by purchasing games that resonate deeply after sampling. Xbox Game Pass suits players who enjoy variety and discovery. It may not fit those who prefer owning a small collection of favorites long-term or who play only one or two games intensively each year. If your internet connection struggles with streaming or you lack compatible devices, the full experience diminishes. Limitations include no ownership rights, potential removal of titles, and tier-specific restrictions on new releases. Regional availability and content vary. Younger players or families should review parental controls and age ratings independently. The service shines less for competitive esports grinders who need every update or dedicated owners of legacy physical collections. Use this checklist when weighing any subscription, including Xbox Game Pass: Score each item honestly over a trial period. Consistent positives across most categories suggest good ongoing value. How many games are typically available on Xbox Game Pass?
Library size fluctuates. Essential offers around 50 curated titles, Premium reaches over 200, and Ultimate provides 400-500+ across platforms, including partner bundles. Does Xbox Game Pass include day-one releases?
Ultimate and PC Game Pass deliver many first-party and select third-party titles on launch day. Lower tiers add them later, often within a year for Xbox-published games. Can I play Xbox Game Pass games on PC and mobile?
Yes. PC access works through the Xbox app, with full libraries on higher tiers. Cloud streaming supports phones, tablets, and browsers, though quality depends on connection. What happens to my saved progress if a game leaves the service?
Local saves and cloud progress for owned or previously played titles often persist. However, you lose access to the game itself unless you purchase it or wait for re-addition. Is the price increase for Ultimate justified?
It depends on usage. Added day-one volume, enhanced cloud quality, and partner subscriptions provide more content, but the jump to $29.99 monthly feels steep for moderate players. Test with a promotion first. Week 1: Sign up for a promotional offer on your chosen tier. Browse the library and install or stream 4-5 titles that interest you. Track time spent and note any technical issues. Focus on different genres to test breadth. Week 2: Dive deeper into 2-3 games. Try cloud play on a secondary device. Review the Rewards dashboard and leaving-soon list. Calculate rough value by comparing against what individual purchases would cost. At the end of two weeks, list finished or abandoned titles, estimate hours played, and compare against the monthly fee. If enjoyment and variety outweigh cost, continue. Otherwise, cancel cleanly and revisit during future promotions or major releases. Decide based on your actual habits rather than hype. For some, the service unlocks exploration they would otherwise skip. For others, selective buying delivers better long-term satisfaction. Either path works when it matches how you actually play. title: GS Global CEO buys shares to signal growth confidence - koreaherald.com
content:
Business GS Global CEO buys shares to signal growth confidence GS Global said Wednesday its newly appointed CEO Kim Sung-won has purchased company shares on the open market, signaling confidence in the firm’s long-term growth prospects. Kim acquired 40,000 shares shortly after the company’s annual general meeting on March 26, according to the company. “The decision reflects my confidence in the company’s future growth,” Kim said, adding that he would continue efforts to enhance shareholder value and strengthen responsible management. The company said the purchase sends a signal to the market about management’s belief in its medium- to long-term growth potential, noting that other executives and employees are also expected to participate in voluntary share purchases. The move comes as GS Global reshapes its leadership structure. At the shareholders meeting, Kim was officially appointed as CEO, while Chief Financial Officer Kim Jae-sung was newly named an inside director. Non-executive director Kim Seok-hwan and outside director Wi Sung-ho were reappointed, and Shin Chang-dong joined the board as a new outside director, strengthening its expertise and independence. GS Global posted consolidated revenue of 4.11 trillion won ($2.7 billion) and operating profit of 52.3 billion won last year, maintaining stable performance despite global trade restrictions and slowing economic growth. However, with most of its revenue concentrated in trading and distribution, the company said diversifying its business portfolio and securing new growth engines remain key priorities. For this year, GS Global has set a revenue target of around 4.6 trillion won, aiming to achieve both top-line expansion and improved profitability. The company also reaffirmed its shareholder-friendly policies, announcing a cash dividend of 25 won per share to be paid on April 3. Looking ahead, GS Global plans to move beyond its traditional trading-focused business model and transform into a “total solution provider,” offering integrated services that identify and address customer needs more proactively. The project, which is promoted by the Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport, is a project to secure base technologies for all cycles, such as storage, transfer, and unloading, and to link demonstration to build a liquid hydrogen acquisition base in preparation for the spread of the hydrogen economy. The total project cost is 29 billion won. GS E&C will design a liquid hydrogen storage tank and develop a drip chamber system in this project, especially for the first time in Korea to develop a flat-floor liquid hydrogen storage tank technology. This is expected to be a technical foundation that can be expanded to demonstrate large-capacity storage systems in the future. 14 industry-academic institutions will participate in this project to improve technology completion. Until now, GS E&C has been preparing for the hydrogen business based on its plant design, procurement, and construction (EPC) capabilities. Last year, it signed an agreement with the Korea Gas Corporation to introduce liquid hydrogen, and is also pursuing storage technology cooperation with Japan's Evara and GS Global. Gmarket was the only one among the eight apps to record a decline, with a decrease of 0.4%. title: GS E&C Selected to Lead National Liquid Hydrogen Storage Tech Project - 서울경제
content: Selected as Lead Institution for Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport National Research Project GS Engineering & Construction (GS E&C) has been selected as the lead institution for a national project to develop liquid hydrogen storage tanks and loading/unloading systems, promoted by the Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport (MOLIT). According to GS E&C on the 1st, the core objective of the national project is to secure fundamental technologies spanning the entire cycle — storage, transfer and unloading — needed to build liquid hydrogen receiving terminals, and to link these with demonstration projects. The initiative prepares for the future expansion of the hydrogen economy, with a total project budget of approximately 29 billion won ($21 million). GS E&C will carry out the design of liquid hydrogen storage tanks, the development of loading and unloading systems — the process of transferring liquid hydrogen between vessels, transport vehicles and storage facilities — and demonstration linkages. The company plans to pursue Korea's first development of flat-bottomed liquid hydrogen storage tank technology, a structure with a flat base suited for large-capacity storage. The project is significant in that it lays a technological foundation that can be expanded into large-capacity liquid hydrogen storage system demonstrations in the future, the company said. A total of 14 institutions, including GS E&C, are participating in the national project. "We will deliver results that go beyond technology development to demonstration and commercialization," a GS E&C official said. GS E&C plans to expand from its traditional petrochemical-focused plant business into the clean energy sector and strengthen its competitiveness in the global race to build hydrogen supply chains. GS Global Corp. will continue its shareholder return policy. On April 3, the company plans to pay a cash dividend of 25 won per share. The move comes as the Istanbul-based club continues to build on its recent sporting and financial momentum, including strong performances in both the Turkish Süper Lig and European competitions. Club officials confirmed that the GS Kafe concept will be rolled out nationwide, in all 81 provinces of Türkiye. The first outlets are expected to open at key symbolic locations, including RAMS Park — the club’s home ground in Sarıyer district — and Istiklal Avenue, one of Istanbul’s busiest cultural and commercial corridors. The cafes will be integrated into existing retail infrastructure, either located within or adjacent to GS Store outlets, with the aim of offering what the club describes as a “fully immersive yellow-red experience” for supporters. The franchise model will require an entry fee of approximately $50,000, enabling rapid domestic expansion while generating licensing revenue for the club. Financially, the project is expected to complement Galatasaray’s already robust merchandising arm, which reportedly surpasses 100 million euros ($115,000,000) in annual revenue. title: NH Securities lifts GS E&C target as cost stabilization boosts outlook - CHOSUNBIZ - Chosunbiz
content: NH Investment & Securities on the 1st said GS Engineering & Construction's construction institutional sector results will improve through cost stabilization. It maintained its "Buy" investment rating and raised its target price to 35,000 won from 30,000 won. The previous trading day's closing price for GS Engineering & Construction was 25,100 won. Lee Eun-sang, an analyst at NH Investment & Securities, judged that this year GS Engineering & Construction will see improved results based on cost stabilization. The analyst said, "The architecture and dwellings institutional sector will contribute to further improvement in the cost ratio and companywide profit as high-cost sites started between 2020 and 2022 are sequentially completed." The plant construction institutional sector also appears likely to show a trend of cost stabilization. The analyst said, "Cost stabilization is expected as the progress rates rise at domestic sites such as the Northeast Asia liquefied natural gas (LNG) hub terminal and LG Chem's Aurora project." Accordingly, the analyst forecast this year's operating profit at GS Engineering & Construction at 540 billion won, up 23% from a year earlier. A foundation has also been laid for expanding new businesses through improved financial soundness. The analyst explained, "The proceeds from the sale of GS Inima are scheduled to flow in during the second half of this year." GS Inima, GS Engineering & Construction's water treatment subsidiary, was sold to TAQA, the UAE state energy company, for 1.68 trillion won. The sale proceeds will first be used to repay liabilities. After that, GS Engineering & Construction is expected to push a business reorganization focused on expanding new businesses. The analyst said, "The disposal proceeds will be prioritized for liability repayment, contributing to stronger financial soundness," and evaluated, "It is also positive that after the sale of GS Inima, the company will be reorganized around high value-added new businesses such as modular, overseas urban development, data centers, and nuclear power." Days 6-7: List three specific questions tied to your goals. Week 2 shifts to outreach. Days 8-10: Contact relevant networks, such as football scouts, travel forums, or business associations. Days 11-12: Schedule informational calls or virtual meetings. Days 13-14: Summarize findings and decide on a small next action, whether booking a exploratory trip or launching a pilot inquiry. Track progress in a simple notebook or digital note. Revisit the plan after one month with updated information. This measured approach builds confidence without premature commitment. Senegal vs Peru ultimately reveals more similarities than differences at first glance: both nations hustle amid global pressures, value resilience, and harbor ambitions beyond current standings. The distinctions emerge in execution details and chosen paths forward. Approach with curiosity, patience, and a willingness to update assumptions as realities evolve. The insights gained often prove more valuable than any single verdict. Country: United States, Australia, United Kingdom. Dynamically explore and compare data on law firms, companies, individual lawyers, and industry trends. Takeaways from the conference on the dual role of courts in protecting privacy rights and the struggle to build a Trans-Atlantic data transfer regime. The second day of the International Association of Privacy Professionals 2026 Global Summit in Washington, D.C., began with talk of “privacy’s identity crisis” and ended with a discussion of unresolved data transfer issues between the U.S. Conversations about privacy tend to focus on the practices of for-profit companies and the rules legislatures and regulatory agencies impose upon them, but courts also have an important role to play in preserving the privacy rights of citizens. The IAPP GS day one session “Privacy Perspectives from the Federal Bench” touched on the power jurists have to remove information revealed at trial from public view, as well as the role they play in determining what investigative steps governments can take in the first place. “Privacy these days is such a weird thing,” said U.S. District Judge Allison Burroughs of the District of Massachusetts. “As Americans, ... especially since 9/11, we’ve all been super quick to give up our privacy in lots of important ways.” However, while Americans may be cavalier about sharing personal data in other contexts, she said they often become more reticent at the courthouse door. U.S. District Chief Judge James Boasberg of the District of Columbia said the reasons parties want information sealed are often understandable, even if not persuasive. “Almost everybody suing about a private matter, a personal matter, ... they don’t want that public,” he said. Conversely, except in notable cases that attract media attention, “There’s no member of the public filing in your case, saying, ‘We want open proceedings.’’’ Because of this imbalance, Boasberg and Burroughs said it can be easy to default to sealing records where the parties agree to do so. Given their role in an adversarial justice system is to settle disputes, judges can be inclined to agree to seal information if neither party is opposed, relieved to have one less issue to weigh in on. However, doing so cuts against the judiciary’s broad aim to provide public access to the court system, which requires judges to factor in considerations beyond the desires of the parties. The judicial role also comes up in the context of approving or rejecting government requests to impose surveillance on criminal suspects. Judicial discretion in this area is particularly important, as the statutory regime governing technology lags far behind the evolution of communication and surveillance technologies in recent decades. "It’s not keeping up, it never has kept up, it never will keep up,” Burroughs said. In some contexts, the ability to exercise judgment in these areas requires the participation of outside technical experts to help educate judges and juries on the tools and techniques involved in an investigation or case. Boasberg noted that the U.S Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Court (FISC), of which he was previously the presiding judge, has a process to allow “technical amici,” typically computers science professors and other technical experts, to explain how the technologies used in pursuit of foreign intelligence actually work. Even outside the anomalous circumstances of the FISC, courts sometimes need to rely on experts provided by the parties or brought in on the court's own initiative to help them understand certain technical aspects of a proceeding. One of the sessions that marked the end of day two of the conference hit on the seemingly endless struggle to develop a U.S.-E.U. data protection framework that sticks. After both the International Safe Harbor Privacy Principles and the E.U.-U.S. Privacy Shield were struck down by the European Court of Justice in response to cases brought by Austrian Privacy activist Max Schrems, the parties developed the E.U.-U.S. Data Privacy Framework (DPF). In a fireside chat, Schrems laid out some of the continuing conflicts he sees between U.S. surveillance laws and European data protections. At the crux of the conflict is the fact that U.S. data protection laws generally only apply to U.S. citizens, in contrast to European laws that apply data protection rights universally. While the DPF set up a Data Protection Review Court to handle appeals from European citizens who feel their rights were violated by American surveillance activities, because the body is not a section 3 court, but instead is housed in the executive branch, it may not constitute an “impartial” tribunal as required under section 47 of the European Convention on Human Rights. Schrems noted that a proper treaty between the parties, coupled with American legislation that extends U.S. data protection laws to cover foreigners, would be preferable to either the present framework or an increasing digital fracture between the U.S. and Europe. However, he added that the massive distrust European governments feel toward the current U.S. presidential administration means any such move is likely some way off. The subject also may become somewhat less pressing if European countries continue to move toward “data sovereignty,” building an indigenous digital structure outside of the control of American companies. A number of forces have contributed to a growing interest in doing so, including privacy concerns, a desire to boost European competitors to American tech companies, and fears of being cut off from digital infrastructure during an international dispute. © 2026 ALM Global, LLC, All Rights Reserved. Join over 9000 global opinion formers by subscribing to Fathom's influential Weekly Newsletter Duncan Robinson has carved out a unique place in the NBA as one of the most efficient long-range shooters of his generation. Now in his eighth season, the 31-year-old forward suits up for the Detroit Pistons after spending his first seven years with the Miami Heat. His story stands out because he went undrafted in 2018 yet built a career around a singular skill: knocking down threes at a high volume and percentage. Fans and analysts often discuss Duncan Robinson when debating the modern NBA's emphasis on spacing and floor-stretching wings. He doesn't dominate with athleticism or create his own shot like a superstar. Instead, he thrives in systems that value movement, off-ball screens, and catch-and-shoot opportunities. In the 2025-26 season with Detroit, he averages 11.9 points per game on 44.4% shooting from the field and 40.4% from three, while playing about 27.6 minutes per night. His path from Division III college basketball to the NBA Finals offers lessons for players, coaches, and even casual observers about persistence and specialization. Robinson's game has clear strengths and limitations, which makes him a fascinating case study in role-player value. Duncan McBryde Robinson was born on April 22, 1994, in York, Maine. He stands 6'7" and weighs 215 pounds. He played high school ball at Phillips Exeter Academy before starting his college career at Williams College, a Division III program. There he put up impressive numbers as a freshman, scoring over 17 points per game. He transferred to the University of Michigan for his final two seasons, where he contributed to competitive teams and improved his three-point shooting. Undrafted out of Michigan, he signed a two-way contract with the Miami Heat. After dominating the G League with the Sioux Falls Skyforce, he earned a standard NBA deal. The core of Duncan Robinson's value lies in his shooting. He has made over 1,400 three-pointers in his career at around 39.8% accuracy. He once held the record as the fastest player to reach 800 career threes. In Miami, he helped stretch the floor for stars like Jimmy Butler and Bam Adebayo, appearing in two NBA Finals (2020 and 2023). In Detroit, his role has evolved slightly. He starts and contributes as a secondary creator at times, averaging 2.1 assists per game this season. His off-ball movement remains elite, running through screens to create open looks. Teammates benefit because defenses must account for his range, which opens driving lanes elsewhere. Robinson delivers spacing that few players match at his salary level. Teams with strong interior scorers or pick-and-roll creators love having a wing who can punish help defense from 28 feet out. His career three-point percentage hovers near 40%, and he has seasons where he shot above 44%. On offense, he forces rotations. When he sets a down screen or flares off a pin-down, big men or help defenders often hesitate, knowing he can make the shot. This creates advantages in half-court sets and transition. Defensively, the picture is more mixed. Robinson understands team concepts and rotates well in help situations. He is not a physical stopper, however. Opponents frequently target him in switches or isolations, especially against quicker guards or stronger wings. In playoffs, this vulnerability can get exposed when schemes simplify. His durability has generally been solid, though he has dealt with minor injuries, including recent hip concerns. At 31, maintenance becomes part of the equation. The benefits show most clearly in regular-season games where teams play with pace and space. Drawbacks appear in high-stakes playoff series where opponents hunt mismatches relentlessly. One common mistake I see with role players like Robinson is coaches over-relying on their shooting without building schemes to protect them defensively. A coach I know once ran a system that featured a similar sharpshooter heavily in the starting lineup without enough help-side defense or switching rules. In the playoffs, the other team attacked that player every possession. The team adjusted mid-series by hiding him on weaker offensive players and using zone concepts more often. They survived, but it was a stressful learning experience that cost them early momentum. Analytics have reshaped how teams value three-point shooting. Studies from sources like Basketball-Reference and Cleaning the Glass show that efficient catch-and-shoot threes correlate strongly with offensive rating. Robinson's career effective field-goal percentage on threes often exceeds league averages for wings. Data also highlights the "gravity" effect. Players who command defensive attention from deep range boost teammate efficiency even when they don't touch the ball. Advanced metrics like hustle stats and off-ball screens attempted put Robinson in solid company among specialists. On the flip side, defensive metrics like defensive box plus-minus or RAPTOR often rate him below average. Teams that reach deep into the playoffs tend to need versatile defenders who can guard multiple positions without creating exploitable weaknesses. Evidence remains nuanced. Some contenders have succeeded with shooting specialists by pairing them with elite defenders (think of past Heat teams). Others have struggled when the specialist becomes a liability in crunch time. Robinson's playoff numbers dip slightly to about 8.5 points per game on lower minutes, reflecting tighter defenses and targeted schemes. No single data set predicts success perfectly. Context—teammates, coaching scheme, opponent personnel—matters enormously. If you're a coach, front office member, or even a fantasy player evaluating similar profiles, start with fit. Ask whether your roster has creators who can generate open threes. Robinson excels when others handle the ball and he moves without it. For fans tracking careers, follow advanced stats beyond points and rebounds. Look at three-point attempt rate, catch-and-shoot percentage, and defensive matchup data from sites like NBA.com or Second Spectrum. Buying into a player like Robinson (whether literally via free agency or figuratively via roster construction) requires balancing offensive upside against defensive risk. Here's a side-by-side look at Robinson against other shooting-oriented forwards and guards in recent seasons. Data reflects career or recent averages for context. Robinson stands out for his size at the position combined with volume shooting. He offers more playmaking than pure spot-up guys like Harris but lacks the elite athleticism of younger wings. Compared to peak Klay, he scores less but fits similar off-ball roles at a lower usage rate. Teams often choose between high-volume specialists and two-way wings. The data suggests Robinson-type players add the most win probability when surrounded by switchable defenders and ball-dominant creators. One frequent error is assuming a hot shooting streak will last. Robinson has had months where he shoots over 45% from deep, followed by colder stretches. Relying on him as a primary scorer without secondary options leads to predictable offenses. Another pitfall involves defensive hiding. Coaches sometimes over-rotate help toward Robinson's man, leaving other areas exposed. The fix is disciplined communication and pre-planned coverage rules. Players emulating his game sometimes neglect footwork or conditioning. Robinson's quick release comes from years of repetition and maintaining body control through screens. Copying the shot without the movement work rarely translates. Avoid the trap of signing or drafting purely on shooting percentages without evaluating off-ball IQ or basketball intelligence. Robinson reads defenses well and finds soft spots in zones. Finally, front offices can undervalue or overvalue specialists during contract years. Data from similar players shows production can fluctuate with role and minutes. This profile of Duncan Robinson may not suit teams needing lockdown perimeter defense or primary scoring options. If your system requires every wing to switch 1-through-5 or create off the dribble consistently, a different archetype fits better. Limitations include age-related decline risks common to all 30-something shooters. Three-point accuracy can hold, but lateral quickness and recovery speed often dip. Injury history, while not extensive, includes nagging issues that affect availability. Robinson's game also depends heavily on scheme. In a slow, iso-heavy offense, his value decreases. He needs space and movement to maximize impact. Use this checklist when assessing players in Robinson's mold: Cross-reference box scores with tracking data for a fuller picture. Single-game heroics mean less than consistent contribution over months. How did Duncan Robinson go from undrafted to NBA contributor?
He dominated the G League after signing a two-way deal, then earned trust with the Heat through consistent shooting and smart play. Hard work in college and summer league paid off. What are Duncan Robinson's career highlights?
Reaching two NBA Finals with Miami, setting three-point records for speed to certain milestones, and maintaining high accuracy across multiple seasons stand out. How has his game changed with the Pistons?
He starts more consistently and handles the ball slightly more, averaging higher minutes and assists while keeping his shooting stroke intact. Is Duncan Robinson a good fantasy basketball option?
He can provide steady threes and points in categories leagues, but his rebounds and defensive stats are modest. Best in builds that reward efficiency. What off-court work is Robinson known for?
He and his family have supported mental health causes, including efforts tied to his brother's experience with schizophrenia. Week 1 focuses on observation. Watch 3-4 recent Pistons games with Robinson on the floor. Note his positioning off the ball, how defenses react, and any adjustments in the fourth quarter. Track his makes and misses in context rather than raw numbers. Spend time reviewing advanced stats on sites like Basketball-Reference. Compare his on/off splits to teammates. Film study of one successful Heat playoff series versus a tougher matchup reveals scheme impacts. Week 2 shifts to application. If you're a coach, diagram two or three sets that mirror what Detroit runs for him. Run them in practice and measure spacing effectiveness. For fans or analysts, write a short breakdown comparing Robinson to another wing on your favorite team. Revisit the evaluation checklist after the two weeks. Adjust your view based on fresh data rather than old narratives. Players like Duncan Robinson reward ongoing attention because their value shifts with roster context and rule changes. His career shows that specialization, when paired with basketball IQ and opportunity, can sustain a long NBA run. Whether you're building a team or simply appreciating the game, studying his approach offers practical takeaways about roles, fit, and adaptation in a three-point-driven league.
